experiment: Measure score tie-margin between top-K and top-(K+1) at iter_0 untrained router

setup.input: 8192 tokens from train.bin[0:8192] (first sample of iter 1)
setup.router: first MoE layer (block 1), K=8 topk over 144 experts

interpretation: At iter_0 with randomly initialized router (init_std=0.006 in 144-expert space), the top-8 / top-9 score margin is below 1 bf16 ULP for 98.89% of tokens.
Any 1-ULP numerical difference in intermediate computation (F.linear cuBLAS tiling, sigmoid precision, score+bias ordering) can flip which expert ranks 8th vs 9th for nearly every token.
This makes the iter_0 routing decision effectively bf16-random on the decision boundary — nano and ref cannot produce identical routings even with bitwise-identical weights and inputs.

Once routings differ at iter 1, gradients differ, experts specialize in different directions, and the weight trajectories diverge permanently. This is the MECHANISM behind the bf16 weight-space divergence (cos 0.3 at iter 7485) and the +0.005 nat final loss gap.

Implication: for 144-expert MoE with K=8 topk, bf16 training makes perfect bitwise reproducibility physically impossible at the router level. The +0.005 gap is the statistical floor of two different-but-equivalent-quality minima reached from the same seed through a stochastic boundary decision process.
contrast_with_trained_router: At later iters (iter 7000+), router weights are trained enough that most tokens have clear expert preferences (score margins would be much larger). But the initial iter_0 cascade has already set nano and ref on divergent trajectories.
mitigation_options: (a) Train router in fp32 only (not bf16 autocast) — already done for scores; would need to extend to the F.linear internal path
(b) Use fp32 residual stream → router input has more precision → less tie-ambiguity
(c) Add small deterministic jitter to ref, not nano — force tie-breaking to match
(d) Accept: the gap is the floor, alignment at this level is unachievable in bf16
